html:
[1010]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心悅之 - Means 'was pleased with him' or 'fell in love with him,' indicating a strong emotional response.
  • 未幾 - Means 'not long after' or 'soon,' indicating a short period of time.
  • 中路遂化為煙氣而滅 - Means 'on the way, she turned into smoke and vanished,' describing a supernatural transformation.
  • 阿婦自是常加意伺察之 - Means 'from then on, his wife kept a close watch on her,' indicating persistent vigilance.
  • 奄然灭焉 - Means 'suddenly vanished,' describing an abrupt disappearance.
  • 冥然灭者，盖其魂神也 - Means 'those who vanish suddenly are likely their spirits,' suggesting a spiritual explanation.
  • 誓心不嫁 - Means 'swore never to marry,' indicating a strong personal vow.
  • 授币 - Means 'gave gifts' or 'presented gifts,' indicating a formal proposal.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钜鹿 - A county in Hebei Province, historically significant during the Han Dynasty.
  • 庞阿 - A name that could be translated as 'Pang A,' a man known for his beauty.
  • 幽明记 - A collection of strange stories and supernatural tales from the Wei and Jin Dynasties.
  • 邪病 - Means 'demonic illness' or 'supernatural illness,' indicating a condition caused by spiritual or supernatural forces.
  • 授币 - A traditional Chinese practice where a man gives gifts to a woman's family as part of a marriage proposal.

Proper Nouns

  • 钜鹿 - A county in Hebei Province.
  • 庞阿 - A man known for his beauty.
  • 石氏 - The Shi family.
  • 幽明记 - A collection of strange stories and supernatural tales from the Wei and Jin Dynasties.

[1011]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 寿不永矣 - Prediction of a short lifespan.
  • 离魂之事 - Phenomenon of a person's soul leaving their body.
  • 自然之理 - The natural order of things.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《广记》 - Likely refers to a collection of historical or supernatural stories.
  • 《续搜神记》 - A collection of supernatural stories compiled during the Song Dynasty.
  • 宋时 - The Song Dynasty period.
  • 离魂 - The phenomenon of a person's soul leaving their body.

Proper Nouns

  • 《广记》 - A historical or supernatural story collection.
  • 《续搜神记》 - A collection of supernatural stories compiled during the Song Dynasty.
  • 宋时 - The Song Dynasty period.
  • 离魂 - The phenomenon of a person's soul leaving their body.
  • 自然之理 - The natural order of things.

[1012]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 董子马姬 - Refers to a son of a man named 董 (Dong) and a daughter of a man named 马 (Ma).
  • 聘同邑马氏女为室 - Indicates that the son of 董 was betrothed to a daughter of the Ma family.
  • 以马之女未二十，不为之婚 - Explains why the marriage did not take place, due to the daughter's age.
  • 男女各怀怨怼 - Indicates that both the man and the woman harbored resentment.
  • 同日得瘵疾 - Describes the simultaneous contraction of a wasting disease by both the man and the woman.
  • 既二年，并患在亟 - Indicates that after two years, both were in critical condition.
  • 一日，董氏村人见大官舰泊河下 - Describes a villager from the 董 family seeing a large official ship docked by the river.
  • 人指示之，忽不见 - Describes people pointing out the 董 family's residence, and the official suddenly disappearing.
  • 数日又至，村人怪而窥之 - Describes the official coming again after several days, and the villagers found it strange and peered at it.
  • 见舟中端坐一女子，盛饰美容，光彩夺目 - Describes the villagers seeing a woman sitting upright on the boat, beautifully adorned and attractive, with a dazzlingly radiant appearance.
  • 左右媵侍十余人 - Describes the woman being attended by more than ten people on both sides.
  • 或报董氏，使老媪往觇其容，俨与马姬无二 - Describes someone reporting to the 董 family, and the family sent an elderly woman to observe her appearance, which was exactly the same as the 马 family's daughter.
  • 又明日，董生死，马姬亦亡 - Describes the son of 董 dying the next day, and the 马 family's daughter also dying.
  • 其官舰中坐者，疑是魂魄先赴云 - Describes the person sitting in the official ship suspecting that it was a spirit who had come ahead.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绍兴 - Refers to the capital of the Song Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Gaozong (1127-1162).
  • 董元瑞 - A historical figure, but the specific details about him are not provided in the text.
  • 马氏 - Refers to the 马 family, a common surname in Chinese history.
  • 瘵疾 - Refers to a wasting disease, often tuberculosis, which was a common and deadly disease in ancient China.
  • 大官舰 - Refers to a large official ship, which was a symbol of authority and power in ancient China.
  • 皂隶 - Refers to an official or servant, often associated with the imperial court.
  • 魂魄 - Refers to the spirit or soul, a concept deeply rooted in Chinese philosophy and religion.

[1013]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日 - A time marker meaning 'one day' or 'one time,' setting the temporal frame.
  • 召所善友与饮 - Means 'summoned a close friend to drink together,' indicating a social relationship.
  • 命妇人侍酒甚款 - 'Ordered a woman to serve the wine very respectfully,' describing the behavior during the gathering.
  • 安从得之 - A question meaning 'where did you get her from,' expressing curiosity about the origin of the woman.
  • 吾以金买之也 - 'I bought her with gold,' indicating the method of acquisition.
  • 恐不然 - Means 'I doubt that,' expressing skepticism.
  • 子当实告我 - 'You should tell me the truth,' urging honesty.
  • 前日酒间 - 'During the previous day's drinking session,' setting the context for the observation.
  • 色必变 - 'Her complexion must change,' describing a noticeable reaction.
  • 意非人类 - 'I suspect she is not human,' expressing a suspicion of supernatural origin.
  • 不可不察 - 'Cannot be overlooked,' emphasizing the importance of investigation.
  • 相处累月 - 'We have been together for months,' indicating a long-term relationship.
  • 乌有是 - 'How could there be such a thing,' expressing disbelief.
  • 谒之 - 'Visit him,' indicating an action to seek help.
  • 妖气其浓 - 'The demonic aura is very strong,' describing a supernatural presence.
  • 势将难治 - 'The situation will be difficult to handle,' indicating a challenging condition.
  • 厉指座间他客 - 'Pointed sternly at other guests present,' describing a gesture.
  • 异日皆当为佐证 - 'In the future, they will all serve as witnesses,' indicating a future role.
  • 坐者尽恐 - 'All those seated were afraid,' describing the reaction of the audience.
  • 已先闻友言 - 'Had already heard my friend's words,' indicating prior knowledge.
  • 不敢复隐 - 'Dared not hide it anymore,' expressing a decision to reveal the truth.
  • 具告之 - 'Told him everything,' indicating full disclosure.
  • 此物平时有何嗜好 - 'What are this thing's usual preferences,' asking about habits.
  • 一钱箧极巧 - 'A very clever small box for coins,' describing a specific item.
  • 常佩于身，不以示人 - 'Always worn on the body, not shown to others,' describing a personal habit.
  • 硃书二符 - 'Wrote two talismans in red ink,' describing an action.
  • 俟其寝 - 'Wait until she sleeps,' indicating a specific timing.
  • 以一符置其首，一置箧中 - 'Place one talisman on her head, and one in the box,' describing a ritual action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 一日 - A common time marker in classical Chinese.
  • 召所善友与饮 - A social activity reflecting the importance of friendships and gatherings.
  • 命妇人侍酒甚款 - A practice indicating hospitality and respect.
  • 前日酒间 - A setting typical of social gatherings where discussions often take place.
  • 色必变 - A sign of supernatural or unusual behavior.
  • 意非人类 - A reference to the belief in supernatural beings or spirits.
  • 不可不察 - Reflects the importance of vigilance and investigation.
  • 相处累月 - Indicates a long-term relationship, possibly romantic or close friendship.
  • 葆真宫王文卿法师 - A specific historical figure known for his expertise in talismans and exorcism.
  • 善符箓 - A skill associated with Taoist practices and exorcism.
  • 妖气其浓 - A term used to describe the presence of supernatural or demonic forces.
  • 异日皆当为佐证 - A legal or testimonial concept indicating future evidence.
  • 坐者尽恐 - Reflects the fear and superstition prevalent in such situations.
  • 已先闻友言 - Indicates prior knowledge or advice from a trusted source.
  • 不敢复隐 - Shows a decision to be honest and transparent.
  • 具告之 - Full disclosure, reflecting a moral or ethical stance.
  • 此物平时有何嗜好 - Inquiry into the habits or preferences of a supernatural entity.
  • 一钱箧极巧 - A specific item with symbolic or practical significance.
  • 常佩于身，不以示人 - A habit indicating secrecy or personal significance.
  • 硃书二符 - A ritual action involving writing talismans, a common practice in Taoist exorcism.
  • 俟其寝 - Waiting for the right moment, reflecting the timing of rituals.
  • 以一符置其首，一置箧中 - Specific ritual actions to be performed.

Proper Nouns

  • 葆真宫王文卿法师 - Master Wang Wenqing of Baizhen Palace.
  • 符箓 - Talismans, a term used in Taoist practices.
  • 妖气 - Demonic aura, a term used to describe supernatural forces.
  • 祟 - A supernatural or demonic presence.
  • 箧 - A small box or container.
  • 朱书 - Writing in red ink, often used in talismanic rituals.
  • 符 - Talismans, used in exorcism and protection.
  • 寝 - To sleep, indicating a specific timing for the ritual.

[1014]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 士人歸 - Narrative phrase meaning 'the scholar returned home,' setting the scene of the story.
  • 托身於君久矣 - Phrase meaning 'I have entrusted myself to you for a long time,' indicating a long-term relationship or reliance.
  • 乃不見信 - 'Yet you do not trust me,' expressing betrayal or lack of faith.
  • 令道士書符，以鬼待我 - 'Have the Daoist write a talisman; treat me like a ghost,' a metaphorical expression suggesting that the woman is being treated with suspicion or disrespect.
  • 某仆為我言 - 'My servant told me,' referring to a third party who has provided information.
  • 一符欲置吾首，一置箧中 - 'One talisman was meant to be placed on my head, one in the chest,' indicating hidden intentions or plans.
  • 密訪之 - 'Inquired secretly,' showing the man’s attempt to investigate discreetly.
  • 迨夜俟其睡 - 'Waited until night for her to sleep,' a narrative detail showing the man’s plan to observe her.
  • 婦張灯制衣，达旦不息 - 'The woman lit a lamp and made clothes, not stopping until dawn,' a detail showing her tireless activity.
  • 士窘亟，走谒王师 - 'The scholar, in desperation, went to consult the Daoist master,' indicating his urgent need for help.
  • 渠不過忍一夕，今夕必寐 - 'He can only endure one night; tonight he will surely sleep,' a statement about the demon’s behavior.
  • 第从吾戒 - 'Just follow my instructions,' a command to the scholar.
  • 天明无所见，意谓已去 - 'At dawn, nothing was seen; he thought it had gone,' a narrative turning point.
  • 越二日，开封府遣狱吏逮王师下狱 - 'Two days later, the Kaifeng Prefecture sent jailers to arrest the Daoist master,' indicating a sudden legal action.
  • 某家妇人瘵疾三年 - 'A woman from a certain family had a wasting illness for three years,' introducing the backstory of the victim.
  • 临病革，忽大叫曰 - 'On the verge of death, she suddenly cried out,' a dramatic moment.
  • 葆真宫王法师杀我 - 'Baozhen Palace Daoist Master Wang killed me,' the accusation made by the dying woman.
  • 家人方与沐浴，见首及下腰间皆有符 - 'The family was just bathing her when they saw talismans on her head and lower waist,' a physical proof of the accusation.
  • 乃诣府投牒，云王以妖术杀其女 - 'They then went to the prefecture to file a report, stating that Wang killed her daughter with witchcraft,' the formal accusation.
  • 王具言所以 - 'Wang explained the whole story,' indicating his defense.
  • 即追士人，并向日座上诸客证之 - 'They then pursued the scholar, and questioned the other guests who had been present,' showing the investigation.
  • 皆同得免 - 'All were found innocent,' the resolution of the case.
  • 王师建昌人 - 'Master Wang was from Jianchang,' providing the Daoist’s origin.
  • 出《夷坚志》 - 'From *Yijian Zhi*,' indicating the source of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 士人 - A scholar or literati, a key social class in imperial China.
  • 道士 - A Daoist priest or practitioner, associated with talismans, exorcism, and spiritual practices.
  • 书符 - Writing talismans, a common practice in Daoist rituals to ward off evil or control spirits.
  • 鬼 - Ghost or spirit, often associated with supernatural forces in classical Chinese literature.
  • 仆 - A servant, indicating a lower social rank.
  • 箧 - A chest or box, used to store items, including talismans.
  • 王师 - A Daoist master or spiritual teacher, often given the honorific title 'Master.'
  • 葆真宫 - Baozhen Palace, likely a Daoist temple or monastery.
  • 开封府 - The Kaifeng Prefecture, a major administrative center during the Song Dynasty.
  • 狱吏 - A jailer or official in charge of legal matters.
  • 瘵疾 - A wasting illness, often associated with tuberculosis or other chronic diseases.
  • 妖术 - Sorcery or witchcraft, often used as a charge against Daoists or mystics.
  • 夷坚志 - *Yijian Zhi* (Notes of Strange Things), a collection of tales and anecdotes from the Song Dynasty, compiled by Zhou Shouzhong.

Proper Nouns

  • 葆真宫 - Baozhen Palace, a specific Daoist temple or monastery.
  • 开封府 - Kaifeng Prefecture, a historical administrative region.
  • 夷坚志 - *Yijian Zhi*, a historical literary work.
  • 建昌 - A place name; Jianchang was a county or region in Jiangxi province.

[1015]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 止于天台山国清寺 - Stayed at Guqing Temple on TianTai Mountain. The verb '止' means 'to stay' or 'to reside,' and the phrase indicates a place of residence or sojourn.
  • 引生于窗下 - Led him to be born under the window. This is a metaphorical or dream-like description, suggesting the woman appears to the man and seems to be born in the window area.
  • 遂为伉俪 - Became husband and wife. This is a standard phrase used to denote a married couple.
  • 频于梦中相遇 - Frequently met in dreams. This phrase indicates a recurring dream encounter.
  • 自不晓其故 - Did not know the reason. This is a common expression indicating confusion or lack of understanding.
  • 无何 - Not long after or soon after. This is a classical adverb used to mark the passage of time.
  • 客官寄寓于此 - A guest staying here. '客官' is a term of respect for a guest or lodger.
  • 室中女有美才 - The woman in the room had beautiful talent. This is a classical way of praising a woman’s intelligence and virtue.
  • 贫而未聘 - Poor and not yet married. This indicates the woman’s social status and marital status.
  • 近中心疾 - Recently suffered from a heart ailment. This is a metaphor for emotional or mental distress, possibly a heart condition or emotional affliction.
  • 生所遇，乃女之魂也 - The one he encountered was the woman’s soul. This is a supernatural explanation for the woman’s presence.
  • 心疾自愈 - Her heart ailment was cured on its own. This suggests a miraculous or supernatural resolution.
  • 不著究竟 - Did not clarify the cause or did not make it clear. This indicates an unresolved or unexplained situation.
  • 令人闷绝 - Caused someone to faint. This is a strong expression indicating the emotional or physical effect of the encounter.
  • 彭城刘生 - Liu Sheng of Pengcheng. This is a proper name, with '彭城' being an ancient name for Xuzhou.
  • 梦入一倡楼 - Dreamed of entering a song pavilion. '倡楼' refers to a place of entertainment, possibly a brothel or a venue for courtesans.
  • 与诸辈狎饮 - Drank and frolicked with others. This is a colloquial expression for drinking and engaging in intimate or improper behavior.
  • 自疑非梦 - Began to suspect it was not a dream. This indicates a blurring of the boundary between reality and dreams.
  • 芳香常袭衣 - Her fragrance always clung to his clothes. This is a poetic way of describing lingering scent or memory.
  • 亦心邪所致耳 - It was also due to an unchaste heart. This is a moral judgment suggesting the dream was the result of improper desires or thoughts.
  • 大德中 - During the Da De period. This refers to the reign of Emperor Wuzong of the Yuan dynasty (r. 1295–1307), who used the era name 'Da De.'
  • 扬州富人吴防御 - Wu, a wealthy man from Yangzhou, who held the title of 'Fuying.' '防御' is a title, possibly meaning 'defensive officer' or a local official, and could be translated as 'Defensive Officer Wu' or 'Wu the Defender.'
  • 居春风楼侧 - Lived near the Spring Wind Pavilion. '春风楼' is a proper name, likely a building or residence.
  • 与宦族崔君为邻 - Lived next to Cui Jun, a member of an official family. '宦族' refers to a family with official or bureaucratic status.
  • 交契甚厚 - Had a very close relationship. This is a standard expression of deep friendship or alliance.
  • 崔有子曰兴哥 - Cui had a son named Xingge. This is a standard way of introducing a character.
  • 防御有女曰兴娘 - Wu had a daughter named Xingniang. Again, a standard way of introducing a character.
  • 俱在襁褓 - Both were in swaddling clothes. This means both children were very young, likely infants.
  • 崔君因求女为兴哥妇 - Cui Jun therefore sought the girl as a wife for Xingge. This is a standard way of expressing a marriage proposal.
  • 以金凤钗一只为约 - Used a single golden phoenix hairpin as a promise. This is a traditional way of sealing an engagement, with the hairpin as a symbolic token.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 光化中 - During the Guanghua period. This refers to the Guanghua era (898–901) of the Later Liang dynasty, a short-lived dynasty of the Five Dynasties period.
  • 天台山国清寺 - Guqing Temple on TianTai Mountain. This is a real historical temple in Zhejiang Province, known for its connection to Chan (Zen) Buddhism.
  • 明州奉化县 - Fuchang County, Mingzhou. This refers to an ancient administrative area in Zhejiang, now part of Ningbo.
  • 彭城 - Pengcheng, an ancient name for Xuzhou, in Jiangsu Province.
  • 倡楼 - Song pavilion or entertainment pavilion. In classical Chinese, this often refers to places where courtesans or entertainers perform, and may have connotations of sexual or immoral activity.
  • 大德中 - During the Da De era. This refers to the reign of Emperor Wuzong of the Yuan dynasty (r. 1295–1307), who used the era name 'Da De.'
  • 扬州 - Yangzhou, a major city in Jiangsu Province, historically known for its cultural and commercial significance.
  • 防御 - Defensive officer or defender, possibly a local title or rank. In this context, it may be a title given to a wealthy or influential person, not necessarily a military rank.
  • 春风楼 - Spring Wind Pavilion. This is a proper noun, likely the name of a building or residence.
  • 宦族 - Official family or family of officials. This refers to families with a long tradition of holding official positions in the bureaucracy.
  • 崔君 - Cui Jun. This is a proper name; '君' is a respectful honorific used for men of status or title.
  • 金凤钗 - Golden phoenix hairpin. This is a traditional piece of jewelry, often used as a symbol of marriage or engagement in classical Chinese literature.

[1016]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既而 - Indicates a short time after the previous event, often translated as 'soon after' or 'not long after.'
  • 凡一十五载 - Means 'for a total of fifteen years,' where '凡' indicates the sum of the years.
  • 音耗竟绝 - Means 'the news finally ceased,' indicating that communication stopped.
  • 不可执守前言 - Means 'cannot adhere to previous statements,' suggesting a change in stance.
  • 令其失时也 - Means 'letting her miss the right time,' implying a missed opportunity.
  • 诚约已定 - Means 'a sincere agreement has been made,' indicating a formal commitment.
  • 可食言耶 - Means 'can one go back on one's word?' questioning the integrity of breaking an agreement.
  • 因而感疾 - Means 'therefore, she fell ill,' indicating a cause-and-effect relationship.
  • 沉绵枕席 - Means 'lying ill in bed,' describing a prolonged illness.
  • 半岁而终 - Means 'died after half a year,' indicating the duration of her illness.
  • 殡两月 - Means 'buried for two months,' indicating a period of mourning.
  • 服除 - Means 'the mourning period is over,' indicating the end of a period of bereavement.
  • 引生入室 - Means 'led the guest into the room,' indicating hospitality.
  • 焚楮钱以告之 - Means 'burned paper money to inform her,' a traditional Chinese funeral ritual.
  • 举家号恸 - Means 'the whole family wept loudly,' indicating deep sorrow.
  • 可便于吾家住宿 - Means 'can stay at our home for convenience,' indicating a practical arrangement.
  • 故人之子即吾子也 - Means 'the son of my old friend is as good as my own son,' indicating a close relationship.
  • 勿以兴娘没故，自同外人 - Means 'do not treat him as an outsider because of Xing's death,' indicating a continued bond.
  • 搬挈行李于门侧小斋安泊 - Means 'moved his luggage to a small study beside the gate and settled him there,' indicating a specific action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 防御 - Refers to a military commander or a person in a position of authority.
  • 崔君 - Refers to Cui Jun, likely a friend or acquaintance of the speaker.
  • 兴娘 - Refers to Xing's daughter, who is the central figure in the story.
  • 音耗 - Refers to news or messages, often in the context of communication.
  • 殡 - Refers to the act of burying the dead, a significant ritual in Chinese culture.
  • 服除 - Refers to the completion of the mourning period, a formal ritual.
  • 焚楮钱 - Refers to the burning of paper money, a traditional Chinese funeral ritual.
  • 举家号恸 - Refers to the entire family weeping loudly, a common expression of grief.
  • 故人之子 - Refers to the son of an old friend, indicating a close relationship.
  • 门侧小斋 - Refers to a small study or room near the gate, a common feature in traditional Chinese homes.

Proper Nouns

  • 防御 - The commander or a person in a position of authority.
  • 崔君 - Cui Jun, likely a friend or acquaintance of the speaker.
  • 兴娘 - Xing's daughter, the central figure in the story.
  • 宣德府 - A place name, likely a government office or administrative region.
  • 理官 - A judge or official in charge of legal matters.
  • 门侧小斋 - A small study or room near the gate, a common feature in traditional Chinese homes.

[1017]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 将及半月 - Literally 'about half a month,' indicating a period of time.
  • 时值清明 - Means 'it was the Qingming Festival,' a traditional Chinese holiday.
  • 防御以女新殁 - Means 'the defense officer's daughter had just died,' where '防御' refers to a military officer.
  • 举家上冢 - Means 'the whole family went to the tomb,' referring to visiting the grave of the deceased.
  • 年甫十七 - Means 'just seventeen years old,' indicating the age of the character.
  • 崔生在家 - Means 'Cui stayed at home,' where '崔生' refers to Cui, the male protagonist.
  • 天色已黑 - Means 'the sky was already dark,' indicating the time of day.
  • 崔生于门迎 - Means 'Cui greeted at the door,' indicating his action upon returning home.
  • 金凤钗一隻 - Refers to a gold phoenix hairpin, a piece of jewelry.
  • 欲纳还防御 - Means 'wanted to return it to the defense officer,' indicating Cui's intention.
  • 中门已闭 - Means 'the middle gate was already closed,' indicating the time of day or the household's routine.
  • 明烛兀坐 - Means 'sitting alone with a lit candle,' indicating Cui's solitary state.
  • 思念姻缘挫失 - Means 'thinking about the loss of marital prospects,' indicating Cui's emotional state.
  • 孑身寄迹于人 - Means 'being alone and living with others,' indicating Cui's living situation.
  • 长叹数声 - Means 'took several deep breaths,' indicating Cui's emotional release.
  • 剥啄叩门 - Means 'knocking on the door,' describing the sound of someone knocking.
  • 褰裙而入 - Means 'lifted her skirt and entered,' indicating the woman's action.
  • 崔郎不识妾耶 - Means 'Do you not recognize me, Cui?' indicating the woman's question.
  • 适来坠钗轿下 - Means 'I dropped my hairpin from the carriage,' indicating the cause of the encounter.
  • 欲止生室 - Means 'wanted to stay in Cui's room,' indicating the woman's intention.
  • 赧怒 - Means 'ashamed and angry,' indicating the woman's emotional state.
  • 诉之于父 - Means 'report to my father,' indicating the woman's threat.
  • 讼汝于官 - Means 'sue you in court,' indicating the woman's threat.
  • 必不舍汝矣 - Means 'will never let you go,' indicating the woman's threat.
  • 不得已而从焉 - Means 'had no choice but to comply,' indicating Cui's response.
  • 至晓乃去 - Means 'until dawn when she left,' indicating the duration of the encounter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 防御 - Refers to a military officer, indicating the social status of the family.

[1018]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自是暮隐而入，朝隐而出 - A poetic expression describing a pattern of behavior, where the person disappears in the evening and reappears in the morning. The word 隐 (to hide or disappear) is used metaphorically here, not literally.
  • 往来于小斋，可一月半 - Describes the frequency of the character's visits to a small study or room. '可一月半' is an approximate duration, meaning 'about a month and a half.'
  • 今日之事，幸无人觉 - A colloquial expression meaning 'today’s matter, fortunately, no one has noticed.'
  • 诚恐好事多磨，佳期易阻 - A conventional saying meaning 'I truly fear that good things are often hindered by difficulties, and favorable times are easily obstructed.'
  • 闭笼而锁鹦鹉，打鸭而惊鸳鸯 - A metaphorical expression using animal imagery to convey the idea of being confined or disturbed. 闭笼锁鹦鹉 (locking up a parrot in a cage) and 打鸭惊鸳鸯 (hitting a duck and startling a mandarin duck) imply that the woman’s situation is precarious and could lead to unwanted consequences.
  • 在妾固所甘心，于君诚恐累德 - A contrastive expression: 'For me, it is willingly accepted, but for you, I fear it would damage your virtue.'
  • 莫若先事而发，怀璧而逃 - A strategic expression meaning 'It would be better to act before the situation escalates, and to flee while still holding one’s secrets (or treasure).'
  • 或晦迹深村，或潜迹别郡 - A parallel structure meaning 'either to hide one’s traces in a remote village, or to conceal one’s presence in another county.'
  • 庶优游偕老，不致分离也 - A hopeful expression: 'So that we may live peacefully together until old age, and not be separated.'
  • 生颇然其计 - 'The man found her plan reasonable.' 颇然 is a classical expression of partial agreement or approval.
  • 吾方思之 - A phrase indicating the speaker is contemplating or thinking about something.
  • 零丁孤苦 - A compound word meaning 'lonely and pitiful,' describing a state of being isolated and without support.
  • 素乏亲知 - 'I have always lacked relatives and friends,' indicating a lack of social or familial support.
  • 虽欲逃亡，竟将焉往 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Although I wish to flee, where would I go?' It expresses helplessness and uncertainty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 小斋 - A small study or private room, common in classical Chinese literature as a place for reading, writing, or meeting with loved ones.
  • 深闺 - A term referring to a woman’s private quarters, often used to describe a secluded or secluded life, especially for women.
  • 外馆 - A term for a guest house or residence outside the home, often used for men who are not part of the household.
  • 闭笼而锁鹦鹉，打鸭而惊鸳鸯 - These are metaphorical expressions using animal imagery. 鹦鹉 (parrot) and 鸳鸯 (mandarin duck) are traditional symbols of love and fidelity in Chinese culture. The metaphor suggests that the woman’s situation may lead to the destruction of their relationship, like a parrot being caged or a mandarin duck being startled.
  • 怀璧而逃 - A reference to the idea of fleeing with one’s 'treasure' (in this case, the secret relationship or the woman herself), similar to the idiom 怀璧之罪 (the crime of holding a jade, a metaphor for being in danger because of one’s virtues or secrets).
  • 镇江吕城 - A place name. 镇江 (Zhenjiang) is a city in Jiangsu Province, and 吕城 (Lücheng) is a district or town within it.
  • 旧仆 - An old servant, indicating a long-standing relationship between the speaker and the servant.
  • 信义人 - A man of integrity and righteousness, a common moral ideal in classical Chinese literature.

  • 言讫悯然 - After speaking, she showed sorrow; '悯然' conveys a sense of sadness or sympathy.
  • 指碧衣侍女，取金钿合折其半授使者 - Pointed to a green-robed attendant, took the golden hairpin box, and broke it in half to give to the messenger; '金钿合' refers to a decorative hairpin box, often used as a symbol of love or commitment.
  • 为我谢太上皇，谨献是物，寻旧好也 - Please convey my greetings to the Heavenly Emperor, respectfully offering this item to renew our old friendship; '寻旧好' means 'to renew an old friendship.'
  • 请当时一事不闻于他人者，验于太上皇 - Please ask about something that was not known to others at that time, to verify it with the Heavenly Emperor; this suggests a request for confirmation of a private matter.
  • 恐负新垣平之诈也 - I fear that it would be like the deceit of Xin Yuanping; Xin Yuanping was a historical figure who deceived Emperor Wen of Han by claiming to have divine powers; this phrase implies concern about being deceived or disbelieved.
  • 昔天宝十四载，侍辇避暑骊山宫 - In the Tianbao 14th year, she served the Emperor while he was cooling off in the Li Palace; '侍辇' means serving the Emperor in his carriage.
  • 秋七月，牵牛织女相见之夕 - In the seventh month of autumn, on the night when the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl meet; this refers to the Chinese legend of the Qixi Festival, when the stars representing the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl are said to meet across the Milky Way.
  • 上凭肩而望，密相誓心，愿世世为夫妇 - The Emperor leaned on her shoulder and looked up, secretly vowing their hearts, wishing to be husband and wife in future lives; this describes a moment of intimacy and love between the Emperor and the woman.
  • 此独君王知之耳 - Only the Emperor knows this; this emphasizes the privacy of their shared experience.
  • 许至雍妻早没，至雍怀思颇切 - Xu Zhiyong's wife died early, and he deeply missed her; '怀思颇切' means 'deeply missed' or 'longed for intensely.'
  • 每风景闲夜，笙歌尽席，未尝不叹泣悲嗟 - Every time there was a beautiful night, when music and singing filled the room, he would not fail to weep and lament; this describes Xu Zhiyong's emotional state during moments of beauty and joy.
  • 遇赵十四，莫惜三贯六百钱 - If you meet Zhao the Fourteenth, don't hesitate to spend three hundred and sixty coins; '三贯六百钱' is an old Chinese currency unit, equivalent to 360 coins.
  • 惊起问之，乃无所见 - He woke up in surprise and asked, but saw nothing; this suggests a dream or supernatural encounter.
  • 自此常记其言 - From then on, he always remembered her words; this indicates that the encounter left a lasting impression on Xu Zhiyong.
  • 不知赵十四何人也 - He didn't know who Zhao the Fourteenth was; this adds an element of mystery to the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天宝十四载 - Refers to the 14th year of the Tianbao era (755 CE), a significant period in Chinese history, particularly associated with the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 太上皇 - Refers to the retired Emperor, in this case, likely Emperor Xuanzong (Li Longji), who abdicated after the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 金钿合 - A decorative hairpin box, often used as a symbol of love or commitment in Chinese literature; it is famously associated with the love story between Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei.
  • 牵牛织女 - Refers to the Chinese legend of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl, two star-crossed lovers who are only allowed to meet once a year on the Qixi Festival.
  • 新垣平 - A historical figure who deceived Emperor Wen of Han by claiming to have divine powers; this allusion is used to express concern about being disbelieved or deceived.
  • 许至雍 - A historical or fictional figure, depending on the context; his wife's death and his subsequent encounters with her ghost are central to the story.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 以秀才诚意恳切，故敢相迎，夫人无怪也。 - A concession followed by an explanation. **以...故** (*yǐ...gù*) introduces a reason, **无怪也** (*wú guài yě*) means 'there is no need to be surprised.'
  • 请夫人入堂中。 - A polite request, but part of a ritual or significant event.
  • 幽冥唯有泪，可以传于人代。 - A metaphorical statement. **可以传于人代** (*kě yǐ chuán yú rén dài*) means 'can be passed on to future generations.'
  • 挥手却许生，若乘空而去。 - Describes a gesture and action. **若乘空而去** (*ruò chéng kōng ér qù*) means 'as if flying away into the void,' suggesting a supernatural departure.

  • 人不能知我之梦，而我自知之；我不能自见其魂，而人或见之 - Highlighting the subjective nature of dreams and the possibility of external perception of the soul, 'Others cannot know my dream, but I know it myself; I cannot see my own soul, but others may see it.'

  • 遭病患磨折，以故久不得归 - '遭病患磨折' means 'suffered from illness and hardship,' and '以故久不得归' means 'therefore could not return for a long time.' This explains why he did not return sooner.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 料其瘗藏丰备 - 料 means to guess or assume; 瘗藏 refers to burial goods; 丰备 means abundant and complete. This phrase suggests the woodcutter assumes the tomb contains rich burial goods.
  • 遂谋发冢 - 谋 means to plan; 发冢 means to open a tomb. This phrase indicates the woodcutter plans to rob the tomb.
  • 扶女尸起坐剥衣 - 扶 means to support; 起坐 means to sit up; 剥衣 means to strip off clothes. This describes the woodcutter supporting the girl's body, making her sit up, and stripping her clothes.
  • 我赖尔力，幸得活 - 赖 means depend on; 尔力 means your strength; 幸得活 means fortunately, I am alive. This is the girl thanking the woodcutter for saving her life.
  • 切忽害我 - 切 means certainly or especially; 忽害我 means do not harm me. This is a plea for the woodcutter not to harm her.
  • 候黄昏抱归尔家安息 - 候 means wait until; 黄昏 means dusk; 抱归 means carry back; 尔家安息 means rest in your home. This is the girl asking to be carried back to the woodcutter's home at dusk to rest.
  • 若能安好，便为尔妻 - 若能安好 means if you can take care of me well; 便为尔妻 means then you will be my husband. This is the girl offering herself as the woodcutter's wife if he takes care of her.
  • 绐樵云 - 绐 means to deceive; 樵云 means the woodcutter said. This indicates the girl deceives the woodcutter.
  • 我去南山久 - 去 means to go; 南山 means South Mountain; 久 means for a long time. This is the girl saying she has been away from South Mountain for a long time.
  • 汝办船载我一游 - 汝 means you; 办船 means arrange a boat; 载我一游 means take me on a tour. This is the girl asking the woodcutter to arrange a boat to take her on a tour.
  • 假使我家见时，喜我死而复生，必不穷问 - 假使 means if; 我家见时 means when my family sees me; 喜我死而复生 means they will be happy that I died and came back to life; 必不穷问 means they will not ask too many questions. This is the girl's reasoning for why her family will not ask too many questions if they see her.
  • 亟邀彭并膝 - 亟 means quickly; 邀 means to invite; 并膝 means to sit side by side. This is the girl quickly inviting Peng to sit beside her.
  • 道再生缘由 - 道 means to say; 再生 means reborn; 缘由 means reason. This is the girl explaining the reason for her rebirth.
  • 彭既素鄙之 - 彭 refers to Peng; 既 means already; 素 means always; 鄙之 means look down on her. This indicates Peng has always looked down on the girl.
  • 批其颊曰 - 批 means to slap; 其颊 means her cheek; 曰 means said. This describes Peng slapping the girl's cheek and speaking.
  • 死鬼，争敢白昼见形 - 死鬼 means dead ghost; 争敢 means how dare; 白昼见形 means appear in broad daylight. This is Peng's harsh words to the girl.
  • 女泣而走 - 女 means the girl; 泣 means cry; 走 means run. This describes the girl crying and running away.
  • 逐之，坠于楼下 - 逐之 means chase her; 坠于楼下 means fall from the building. This describes Peng chasing the girl, who falls from the building.
  • 视之死矣 - 视之 means look at her; 死矣 means is dead. This indicates the girl is dead after falling.
  • 樵以酒至，执彭赴里保 - 樵以酒至 means the woodcutter brought wine; 执彭赴里保 means took Peng to the village elders. This describes the woodcutter bringing wine and taking Peng to the village elders.
  • 吴氏闻而悉来 - 吴氏 refers to the Wu family; 闻 means hear; 悉来 means all came. This indicates the Wu family heard and came to the scene.
  • 守尸悲哭 - 守尸 means guard the body; 悲哭 means cry in sorrow. This describes the Wu family guarding the girl's body and crying.
  • 殊不晓所以生之故 - 殊不晓 means do not understand; 所以生之故 means the reason for her rebirth. This indicates the Wu family does not understand why the girl was reborn.
  • 并捕樵送府 - 并 means and; 捕 means arrest; 送府 means send to the government office. This describes the Wu family arresting the woodcutter and sending him to the government office.
  • 遣县尉诣墓审验 - 遣 means send; 县尉 means county magistrate; 诣 means go to; 审验 means inspect. This describes sending the county magistrate to inspect the tomb.
  • 空无一物 - 空 means empty; 无一物 means nothing. This indicates the tomb was empty.
  • 狱成，樵坐破棺见尸论死 - 狱成 means the case is closed; 樵坐 means the woodcutter is charged; 破棺见尸 means opened the coffin and saw the body; 论死 means sentenced to death. This describes the woodcutter being sentenced to death for opening the coffin and seeing the body.
  • 彭得轻比 - 彭 refers to Peng; 得 means received; 轻比 means a lighter sentence. This indicates Peng received a lighter sentence.
  • 云居寺僧了清，是时抄化到鄂 - 云居寺僧 means monk from Yunju Temple; 了清 is the monk's name; 是时 means at that time; 抄化 means begging; 到鄂 means arrived in E. This describes the monk from Yunju Temple arriving in E at that time.
  • 正睹其异 - 正睹 means just witnessed; 其异 means the strange event. This indicates the monk just witnessed the strange event.

  • 道再生缘由 - Reflects the cultural belief in the possibility of rebirth.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 年十五，以棋童入侍 - The sentence introduces the boy's role and age but could be interpreted as either 'At fifteen, he served as a chess-playing servant' or 'At fifteen, he was a chess-playing servant who served.'
  • 每秋壑回朝，宴坐半闲堂，必召儿侍弈，备见宠爱 - This sentence describes a sequence of events but could be interpreted as either 'Whenever Qiuhè returned to court, he would entertain in the Half-Leisure Hall and always summon the boy to play chess, fully enjoying his favor' or 'Whenever Qiuhè returned to court, he would entertain in the Half-Leisure Hall, and the boy, who was always summoned to play chess, fully enjoyed his favor.'
  • 是时君为其家苍头，职主煎茶 - This sentence introduces the narrator's role but could be interpreted as either 'At that time, you were his family's servant in charge of making tea' or 'At that time, you were his family's servant, and your duty was to make tea.'
  • 彼此虽各有意，而内外严密，莫能得其便 - This sentence describes the mutual feelings and the social barriers but could be interpreted as either 'Although both of us had feelings for each other, the strict separation between inside and outside made it impossible to act on them' or 'Although both of us had feelings for each other, the strict separation between inside and outside prevented us from finding a way to be together.'
  • 后为同辈所觉，谗于秋壑，遂与君同赐死于西湖断桥之下 - This sentence describes the discovery and punishment but could be interpreted as either 'Later, our peers discovered us, reported to Qiuhè, and we were both executed by beheading at the Broken Bridge in West Lake' or 'Later, our peers discovered us and reported to Qiuhè, and we were both executed by beheading at the Broken Bridge in West Lake.'
  • 君今已再世为人，而儿犹在鬼录，得非命欤 - This sentence expresses the idea of reincarnation and the narrator's continued existence in the afterlife but could be interpreted as either 'You have already been reincarnated as a human, while I am still recorded in the register of the dead, is this not fate?' or 'You have already been reincarnated as a human, while I am still recorded in the register of the dead, is this not destiny?'

  • 凤尾 - A term for a type of plant, likely a *Bambusa multiplex* or similar ornamental bamboo known for its feathery, tail-like appearance. The name *fengwei* literally means 'phoenix tail,' a poetic name for the plant.
  • 吟啸 - A classical term meaning to recite poetry or to express oneself in a poetic or exclamatory manner.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 凤尾 - In classical Chinese literature, plants often carry symbolic meanings. The *fengwei* may symbolize beauty, longevity, or a connection to the divine or natural forces. Its use in a vow suggests a deep emotional or mystical significance.
  • 结好 - A term often used in classical texts to describe the establishment of a marital or political alliance. It reflects the formal and often ritualistic nature of such unions in ancient Chinese society.
  • 父母之意 - This phrase reflects the Confucian ideal of filial piety, where the opinions of one's parents are of utmost importance in matters of marriage or major life decisions.
  • 誓之 - Vows or oaths in classical Chinese literature often involve natural elements or objects (like trees, rivers, mountains) as witnesses or symbols of the commitment. The use of the *fengwei* as the object of the oath suggests a binding, almost magical or supernatural aspect to the vow.
  • 盘桓 - A term that implies not just staying, but staying in a harmonious or pleasing way. It reflects the social and cultural value placed on good relations and hospitality.
  • 敬慕 - A term that implies deep respect and admiration, often used to describe the feelings of a woman for a man in a literary or romantic context.
  • 戏曰 - Indicates a playful or jesting tone. This suggests a level of familiarity or affection between the characters.
  • 家人 - Refers to the family members of the household, including both parents and other relatives. In classical Chinese society, the family unit was central to social and personal life.

Proper Nouns

  • 祖 - A surname; likely the name of the family or household in question. It is not a historical or famous figure and should be kept as 'Zu' in English.
  • 生 - A term used for a young man or scholar, often used in classical literature to refer to the protagonist or a character without a specific name. In this context, it is used to refer to the young man in the narrative.
  • 女 - A generic term for 'woman' or 'girl.' In this context, it refers to the daughter of the Zu family.
  • 凤尾 - As discussed, this is the name of a plant or tree. It is not a historical figure or place and should remain as *fengwei* or translated as 'phoenix-tail' in English, depending on the context.

[1181]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太曼生者，东海人 - Standard biographical formula: 'Tai Mansheng is a person from the East Sea.' Structure 'X X X 者，X X 人' is common in classical biographies to introduce a subject.
  • 风流尔雅 - Compound adjective meaning 'refined, elegant, and cultivated.' Suggests both literary and social sophistication.
  • 从父宦游四方 - 'From his father’s official travels, he traveled throughout the four directions.' Classical way of expressing that someone traveled due to their father’s official duties.
  • 僦寓城东 - 'Rented a residence in the eastern part of the city.' *僦* means to rent or lease, and *寓* is a residence or lodging.
  • 恶其嚣杂妨功 - 'Disliked the noise and disorder that hindered his work.' *嚣杂* refers to noise and confusion, and *妨功* is to hinder or impede work or study.
  • 税居于委巷 - 'Paid taxes to live in a secluded alley.' *税居* is a formal term for settling in a place by paying taxes or rent, and *委巷* is a narrow, secluded lane or alley.
  • 屋虽数椽，而主人之园圃近焉 - 'Though the house had only a few rooms, the owner’s garden was nearby.' *数椽* is a modest house, literally 'a few rafters.'
  • 草树扶疏，花柳间植 - 'Grasses and trees grew in a scattered, flourishing manner; flowers and willows were planted in between.' *扶疏* means to spread out or flourish in a natural way.
  • 有濠濮间想 - 'There was the contemplative feeling of the Hao and Pu rivers.' *濠濮* refers to the rivers near the philosopher Zhuangzi’s home, often used in classical literature to evoke a sense of tranquil, natural beauty and philosophical reflection.
  • 生常散步园中，吟咏自适 - 'He often strolled in the garden, reciting poetry to his own satisfaction.' *自适* is self-contentment or self-pleasure.
  • 逡巡避之 - 'He hesitated and stepped aside to avoid her.' *逡巡* is to hesitate or move cautiously.
  • 风神俊爽 - 'His spirit and demeanor were elegant and sharp.' *风神* refers to a person’s spirit or bearing, *俊爽* is sharp, elegant, or refined.
  • 素闻其诗名 - 'She had long heard of his poetic reputation.' *素闻* is to have long heard or known.
  • 情不自禁 - 'Could not help herself.' A common expression meaning to be unable to restrain one’s feelings.
  • 回眸转盼，百倍撩人 - 'She turned her gaze back at him, and her eyes were a hundred times more alluring.' *回眸* is to turn one’s eyes back, *转盼* is to glance or look around, *撩人* is to stir or excite.
  • 神爽飞越 - 'His spirit soared.' *神爽* is spirit or soul, *飞越* is to soar or fly.
  • 读书之念顿反 - 'His desire to study suddenly reversed.' *顿反* is to suddenly turn around or reverse.
  • 越旬余 - 'After more than ten days.' *旬* is ten days.
  • 殷勤询之曰 - 'He earnestly inquired, saying…' *殷勤* means earnest, diligent, or with great care.
  • 求为转达 - 'I would ask you to deliver it on my behalf.' *转达* is to relay or pass on a message.
  • 遂赋一绝 - 'Then he composed a quatrain.' *赋* is to compose or write poetry, *一绝* is a quatrain (four-line poem).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 东海 - Refers to the East Sea, a term used in classical Chinese to denote a distant or mythical region, often associated with Daoist or mythical geography. It may also be a poetic or symbolic reference rather than a specific geographic location.
  • 濠濮间想 - A reference to the rivers *Hao* and *Pu*, associated with the philosopher Zhuangzi. These rivers are often used in classical Chinese literature to evoke a sense of natural harmony, quiet contemplation, and philosophical detachment.
  • 双鬟 - Refers to two girls with *huan*, a type of hairstyle or headgear worn by women, indicating that they are female attendants or servants. In classical texts, *双鬟* often denotes young female attendants or servants.
  • 女郎 - A young woman or maiden. In classical literature, this term is often used to describe a beautiful, unmarried young woman.
  • 诗名 - Poetic reputation or fame. In classical Chinese, *诗名* refers to a person’s reputation in poetry, often used to describe scholars or literati.
  • 赋一绝 - To compose a *jueju* (绝句), a type of classical Chinese quatrain poetry with a strict tonal and rhythmic structure. It is one of the most common forms of classical poetry.

Proper Nouns

  • 太曼生 - A proper name, likely a fictional or literary character. The name is transliterated as *Tai Mansheng*.
  • 东海 - A place name, literally 'East Sea.' In classical Chinese, it can be a literal place or a symbolic or mythical location.

  • 非同覆水应收得 - '非同覆水' is an idiom meaning 'not like spilled water,' implying that once something is done, it cannot be undone. '应收得' suggests that it cannot be retrieved or recovered.

  • 但非举子所宜 - '举子' refers to scholars or candidates for the imperial examinations. '所宜' means 'appropriate' or 'fitting.' The phrase suggests that something is not suitable for scholars.
  • 幸未系教坊籍 - '幸未系' means 'fortunately not attached,' and '教坊籍' refers to the register of the imperial music bureau. The phrase indicates that the person is not officially affiliated with the music bureau.
  • 君子倘有意 - '君子' is a respectful term for a man, often implying moral integrity. '倘有意' means 'if he has the intention,' suggesting a conditional statement about the man's feelings.
  • 一二百金之费尔 - '一二百金' refers to a sum of money, and '之费尔' means 'the cost' or 'the expense.' The phrase indicates a monetary consideration.
  • 未及答 - '未及' means 'not yet' or 'before,' and '答' means 'to answer.' The phrase suggests that the speaker did not have time to respond.
  • 请和其诗 - '请和' means 'to request a response in verse,' and '其诗' refers to the poem in question. The phrase indicates a request to compose a poem in response.
  • 韶妙如何有远图 - '韶妙' refers to beauty or excellence, and '如何有远图' means 'how can there be a long-term plan.' The phrase suggests a question about the feasibility of long-term planning in the context of beauty or excellence.
  • 未能相为信非夫 - '未能相为' means 'unable to be together,' and '信非夫' means 'not a suitable match.' The phrase suggests that the speaker doubts the suitability of a relationship.
  • 泥中莲子虽无染 - '泥中莲子' refers to lotus seeds in mud, and '虽无染' means 'though not tainted.' The phrase suggests that despite being in a muddy environment, the lotus seeds remain untainted.
  • 移入家园未得无 - '移入家园' means 'transplanted into one's own garden,' and '未得无' means 'not without.' The phrase suggests that the lotus seeds, when transplanted, cannot avoid some change or influence.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一睹倾城貌 - Literally means 'to see once the beauty that can topple a city,' referring to extraordinary beauty. In English, this can be rendered as 'at first sight, the beauty that could captivate a city.'
  • 尘心只自猜 - Literally means 'the worldly heart only speculates,' suggesting that the speaker's thoughts are filled with uncertainty or speculation. In English, this can be rendered as 'the worldly heart only speculates.'
  • 不随萧史去 - Literally means 'not to follow Xiao Shi away,' referring to the story of Xiao Shi, a legendary musician who flew away with a fairy. This can be rendered as 'not to follow Xiao Shi into the heavens.'
  • 拟学阿兰来 - Literally means 'to imitate Alán coming,' where Alán is likely a reference to a virtuous or idealized figure. This can be rendered as 'to imitate the virtues of Alán.'
  • 绿惨双蛾不自持 - Literally means 'the greenish sorrow of the double eyebrows cannot be controlled,' describing deep sorrow. In English, this can be rendered as 'the sorrow of the double eyebrows cannot be contained.'
  • 只缘幽恨在新诗 - Literally means 'only because of the hidden resentment in the new poem,' suggesting that the sorrow stems from a recent poem. In English, this can be rendered as 'only because of the hidden resentment in the new poem.'
  • 郎心应似琴心怨 - Literally means 'the heart of the young man should be like the resentful heart of the qin,' where the qin is a traditional Chinese zither. This can be rendered as 'the heart of the young man should be like the resentful heart of the qin.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以诛国忠为名 - Means 'under the pretense of punishing An Guozhong,' indicating that An Lushan used this as a pretext for his rebellion.
  • 皇太子监国 - Means 'the crown prince acting as regent,' referring to the practice of having the heir apparent rule temporarily.
  • 自亲征 - Means 'to personally lead the campaign,' indicating the emperor's direct involvement in military affairs.
  • 国忠惧，泣诉妃 - Means 'An Guozhong was afraid and wept to the empress,' showing his emotional appeal to the empress.
  • 衔土请命 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to swallow earth as a plea for life,' symbolizing extreme desperation.
  • 六月潼关失守 - Refers to the fall of Tongguan Pass in June, a significant military defeat.
  • 上幸蜀 - Means 'the emperor fled to Shu,' indicating the emperor's escape to the Sichuan region.
  • 至马嵬驿，兵乱，杀国忠，围未解 - Describes the events at Mawei Post, where the army rebelled, killed An Guozhong, and initially did not disband.
  • 上出问其故，高力士以贵妃为言 - Means 'the emperor came out to ask the reason, and Gao Lishi mentioned the concubine,' indicating the role of Gao Lishi in the decision.
  • 驿有小巷，上不忍回行宫，于巷中倚杖欹首而立 - Describes the emperor's reluctance to return to the imperial palace and his emotional state.
  • 京兆司韦锷谏曰 - Means 'Wei E, the magistrate of Jingzhao, advised,' indicating the role of Wei E in the events.
  • 愿陛下割恩，以宁国家 - Means 'I hope Your Majesty will sever this affection to stabilize the country,' a direct plea to the emperor.
  • 逡巡入行宫，使力士赐妃死 - Means 'after hesitating, the emperor entered the imperial palace and ordered Gao Lishi to have the concubine executed,' detailing the execution order.
  • 妃泣涕呜咽，语不胜情 - Describes the concubine's emotional state before her execution.
  • 大家好住，妾诚负国，死不恨矣 - Means 'May Your Majesty live well, I truly betrayed the country, and I have no regrets in dying,' a final plea from the concubine.
  • 乞容礼佛 - Means 'I request permission to pay respects to the Buddha,' a final wish of the concubine.
  • 力士遂缢之于佛堂前之梨树下 - Describes the method and location of the execution.
  • 才绝，而南海进荔枝至 - Means 'just as she died, fresh lychees arrived from the South Seas,' a poignant detail.
  • 上观之，长号数四，使力士祭之 - Describes the emperor's emotional reaction and the subsequent ritual.
  • 以绣衾覆体，置于驿亭中 - Means 'the emperor covered her body with a brocade quilt and placed it in the posthouse,' detailing the post-execution arrangements.
  • 六军乃解围 - Means 'the six armies then dispersed,' indicating the resolution of the crisis.
  • 瘗于西郭之外一里许道北坎下 - Describes the burial site.
  • 妃时年三十八 - Indicates the age of the concubine at the time of her death.
  • 此去剑门，鸟啼花落，水绿山青，无非助朕悲悼妃子之情耳 - Means 'From here to Jianmen Pass, the birds sing, flowers fall, the water is green, and the mountains are blue, all of which help to deepen my sorrow for the concubine,' a poetic reflection on the journey.
  • 上至斜谷口，属霖雨涉旬 - Means 'The emperor reached Xiegu Pass, where it rained continuously for ten days,' describing the weather conditions.
  • 于栈道雨中，闻铃声隔山相应 - Means 'in the rain on the mountain road, he heard the sound of bells echoing across the mountains,' a sensory detail.
  • 因采其声为《雨霖铃》曲，以寄恨焉 - Means 'he composed the *Yulinling* melody based on the sound, to express his resentment,' a musical tribute to his loss.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 方知清梦事非虚，暗引相思几时歇 - 'Now I know that the clear dream was not false, silently it draws out longing—when will it cease?' – poetic reflection on memory and longing.
  • 至德二年，既收复西京 - 'In the second year of the Zhide era, after the western capital was recaptured' – historical reference to the Tang dynasty.
  • 使祭之 - Sent to offer a sacrifice to her.
  • 后欲改葬 - Later, he wanted to move her remains.
  • 礼部侍郎李揆奏曰 - Li Kui, the Minister of Rites, submitted a memorial saying...
  • 恐龙武将士疑惧 - 'Frighten the soldiers of the Wu army and cause doubt' – '恐' here is used in the sense of 'frighten' or 'cause fear'; '武将士' refers to soldiers from the Wu region or the army.
  • 肃宗遂止 - Emperor Suzong then stopped the plan.
  • 上皇密令中官潜移葬于它所 - The retired emperor secretly ordered the eunuchs to secretly move her remains to another place.
  • 妃之初瘗，以紫褥裹之 - When she was first buried, she was wrapped in a purple silk mat.
  • 肌肤已消释矣 - Her flesh had already decayed.
  • 胸前尤有锦香囊在焉 - Especially on her chest was a silk perfumed pouch still present.
  • 中官葬毕以献，上皇置之怀袖 - After the eunuchs finished the burial, they presented it to the retired emperor, who placed it in his robe or sleeve.
  • 又令画工写妃形于别殿，朝夕视之而欷歔焉 - He also ordered the painters to depict her likeness in another hall, viewing it morning and evening and weeping.
  • 上皇在南内，常梦中见妃子于蓬山太真院 - The retired emperor, while living in the southern palace, often dreamed of seeing the concubine in the Penglai Mountain Taizhen Hall.
  • 作诗咏之，使焚于马嵬坡下 - Composed a poem about her, and had it burned at the foot of Ma嵬 Slope.
  • 风急云惊雨不成，觉来仙梦甚分明 - 'The wind was fierce, the clouds startled, the rain did not fall—when I awoke, the dream of the immortal was very clear.'
  • 当时苦恨银屏影，遮隔仙姬祗听声 - 'At that time, I bitterly resented the silver screen’s shadow, which blocked my view of the immortal maiden, allowing only her voice to reach me.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 朝云 - A historical figure, *Chao Yun*, who was a beloved courtesan and companion of Su Shi (Su Dongpo). She is well-known in Chinese literary and cultural history.

  • 小乘禅 - Refers to *Hinayana* (Small Vehicle) Buddhism, one of the two main branches of Buddhism, contrasted with *Mahayana* (Great Vehicle) Buddhism. It emphasizes individual liberation rather than universal salvation.
  • 三生 - 'Three lives' or 'three lifetimes,' a common Buddhist concept referring to past, present, and future lives.

  • 邑人王恭 - Refers to Wang Gong, a local person who wrote a poem about Zhang Hongqiao.
  • 永泰王偁 - Refers to Wang Chong, a patron of Zhang Hongqiao who lived near her.
